Cook's outlier handling — how the data is filtered

Cook's distance measures how much a single sample pulls a gene's fit. A high value means one replicate is wildly out of step with the rest of its group (e.g. a female sample contaminating an otherwise male group, inflating Xist). This setting decides what the tool does with such genes.

Outlier handling is only active when at least one group has ≥3 replicates (the same gate DESeq2 uses) and there is ≥1 residual degree of freedom. A sample is "influential" when its Cook's distance exceeds the cutoff qF(0.99, 2, m−2) — the 99th percentile of the F-distribution, where m is your total sample count. Below the threshold for replicates, this setting does nothing and every gene is kept.

The four modes, in order of the dropdown
Robust filter (leave-one-out) Recommended
Decides after the normal test and the BH correction have already run, because it needs the real adjusted p-values. The reported log₂FC, p, and padj are never altered — this mode only flips a gene's significant / not-significant flag. For every gene:
  1. Run the standard test on all samples (identical to "Off"). Only genes that come out significant here are even considered.
  2. Find that gene's single worst sample by Cook's distance, computed with the sensitive global trend dispersion. If its Cook's is ≤ the cutoff, the gene is robust → kept.
  3. If it exceeds the cutoff, drop that one sample and re-run the test. (If dropping it would leave that group with fewer than 2 replicates, the gene is kept — there's nothing left to judge with.)
  4. If the gene is still significant without the offending sample → kept. If it now falls below the significance frontier → demoted (marked non-significant).
Because it only ever inspects genes that were already significant, it can only ever remove hits — never add them. The resulting DEG set is always a strict subset of "Off". Demoted genes still appear in plots with their honest all-sample numbers; hovering one shows why it was dropped. The stat bar reports these as … demoted (failed leave-one-out).
DESeq2-faithful (gene-wise Cook's) padj → NA
Decides during detection, before BH. For each gene it computes the maximum Cook's distance using that gene's own shrunken dispersion — the same value used for its Wald test. If that exceeds the cutoff, the gene is flagged: its padj is set to NA and it is pulled out of the multiple-testing set entirely, so it can't be a DEG.
This mirrors stock DESeq2. At ~3 replicates it rarely fires, because a single outlier inflates the gene's own dispersion and effectively masks itself — so it does not flag the noisy-Xist case (matching real DESeq2), but also misses the contamination-driven false positive. Honest, not better. Flagged genes show in the stat bar as … Cook's-flagged (NA padj).
Aggressive NA (trend Cook's) padj → NA
Same mechanism as DESeq2-faithful — flag during detection, set padj to NA, remove from the BH set — but the Cook's distance is computed with the global trend dispersion instead of each gene's own. That dispersion is far more sensitive, so it fires readily.
It catches both Xist cases but also discards legitimate DEGs that simply have one noisy replicate. This was the old, over-eager default; the robust filter exists to get the same sensitivity without throwing away real hits.
Off — keep all
No outlier handling whatsoever. Every gene is tested on all samples and BH-corrected together. This is the baseline that the robust filter is always a subset of.
Quick comparison

Robust only flips significance flags and keeps reported numbers honest; faithful and flag set padj to NA and exclude the gene from multiple-testing. Robust and flag use the sensitive trend dispersion to detect; faithful uses each gene's own dispersion (so it fires least at low replicate counts). All four share the same qF(0.99, 2, m−2) cutoff and the same ≥3-replicate gate.

Building Method

Methods

RNA-seq Explorer runs an entire differential-expression workflow in the browser as a single self-contained HTML file. No server is involved and no count data ever leaves the user's machine; all computation is performed client-side in JavaScript.

Differential expression. Raw counts are pre-filtered by a user-set rowSums threshold, then normalized with the median-of-ratios method to correct for sequencing-depth differences between samples. A gene-wise dispersion is estimated and shrunk toward a fitted mean–dispersion trend, and differential expression is called with a DESeq2-style Wald test. p-values are adjusted across all tested genes by the Benjamini–Hochberg procedure (padj / FDR). log₂ fold change is computed on size-factor-normalized counts with a 0.5 pseudocount; positive values indicate up-regulation in the treatment group relative to the control reference.

Outlier handling. An optional Cook's-distance step identifies samples that disproportionately drive a gene's fit, offering four modes (a robust leave-one-out filter, a DESeq2-faithful gene-wise flag, an aggressive trend-based flag, and off). The threshold follows DESeq2's qF(0.99, 2, m−2) convention and is only active when a group has ≥3 replicates.

Unsupervised views. PCA and the heatmap use a blind variance-stabilizing transform (VST) fitted once across all loaded samples, independent of the group labels. PCA projects the 500 most-variable genes onto the first two principal components by power iteration; the heatmap z-scores each gene across the displayed samples. The expression scatter offers VST, log₂(mean + 1), or linear scaling.

Functional analysis. Pathway over-representation is tested against Reactome (its AnalysisService, with an Enrichr fallback), KEGG, and WikiPathways via a hypergeometric test. Hallmark enrichment is scored from Enrichr results. Transcription-factor activity is inferred from the DEG table using the univariate linear model (ULM) from decoupleR against CollecTRI regulons (Müller-Dott et al., 2023), with a complementary regulon over-representation test.

Annotation. Mouse genes are mapped to MGI symbols, with ~77,600 Ensembl IDs resolved; duplicate symbols can be disambiguated by their Ensembl identifier.

AI Disclosure

This tool was developed with the assistance of Claude (Anthropic), a large language model, which generated the application code. Yaxin Liu specified the analytical requirements and design, conducted testing and debugging, and takes responsibility for the tool's implementation and outputs.
